name: Banking
description: Banking sector process readiness assessment covering onboarding, lending, payments, collections, regulatory reporting, and core operations for Saudi Arabian and GCC financial institutions
persona: You are a senior banking transformation consultant with 22 years of experience across retail banking, corporate banking, Islamic banking, and financial services in Saudi Arabia and the GCC region. You have deep expertise in core banking platforms (Temenos T24, Oracle FLEXCUBE, Path Solutions iMAL, Finastra Fusion), SAMA regulatory frameworks, BIAN service domains, AAOIFI Shari'ah standards, and Basel III compliance as implemented by SAMA. You have led large-scale banking transformation programmes in Saudi Arabia and across the MENA region covering KYC/AML automation under SAMA AML/CFT guidelines, Murabaha and Ijarah financing origination systems, SARIE payment hub modernisation, NPL management under SAMA prudential guidelines, and regulatory reporting automation for SAMA supervisory submissions. You specialise in identifying process inefficiencies, control gaps, and automation opportunities across the full banking value chain — including Islamic finance product workflows — and you benchmark client maturity against SAMA guidelines, Vision 2030 Financial Sector Development Programme (FSDP) objectives, and APQC PCF standards.

broadAreas.name: Customer Onboarding
broadAreas.description: End-to-end customer acquisition lifecycle from KYC through account activation and digital channel enrolment

subAreas.name: KYC / AML
subAreas.description: Know Your Customer and Anti-Money Laundering compliance, screening, and ongoing monitoring under SAMA AML/CFT Guidelines
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank collects and verifies customer identity documents (Saudi National ID, Iqama for expatriates, passport, Absher digital verification), how KYC data is stored and refreshed in line with SAMA AML/CFT Guidelines, what AML screening tools are in use (e.g., Actimize, NICE, Oracle FCCM), how transaction monitoring rules are configured and tuned, what the false positive rate is, how Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) are filed through goAML to SAMA's Financial Intelligence Unit (SAFIU), how re-KYC is triggered and tracked, and how the bank handles centralised KYC data sharing through SIMAH and Nafath digital identity frameworks.

benchmarks.maturity_1: KYC is entirely paper-based; documents are collected physically at branches, photocopied, and stored in physical files; AML screening is done manually against printed watchlists; no systematic transaction monitoring exists; STR filing to SAMA SAFIU via goAML is reactive and infrequent; no integration with Nafath or Absher for identity verification
benchmarks.maturity_2: KYC documents are scanned and stored in a document management system; basic AML screening against static lists is automated but rule sets are rarely updated; transaction monitoring uses basic threshold rules with high false-positive rates; re-KYC is triggered manually and tracked in spreadsheets; Absher verification is available but used only for select customer segments
benchmarks.maturity_3: Digital KYC collection via online portal with Nafath-based identity verification and API integration with Absher for Saudi nationals; AML screening integrates with live watchlist feeds (OFAC, UN, SAMA sanctions lists); transaction monitoring system (TMS) is deployed with configurable rule sets calibrated to SAMA AML/CFT Guidelines; STR workflow is managed in the TMS with goAML submission integration; SIMAH data is queried automatically as part of the KYC workflow
benchmarks.maturity_4: End-to-end remote KYC onboarding using Nafath digital identity with biometric liveness checks and Absher validation; AI-assisted AML risk scoring supplements rule-based screening, reducing false positives by >40%; real-time transaction monitoring with dynamic rule adjustment based on risk appetite; STR filing is automated with pre-populated fields from the TMS and direct goAML API submission; PDPL-compliant data handling with automated retention and deletion workflows
benchmarks.maturity_5: Frictionless KYC using federated digital identity via Nafath and SAMA's Open Banking framework for financial data access; ML models continuously learn from confirmed SAR outcomes to refine risk scores with minimal human review; behavioural analytics detect anomalies beyond traditional rules; perpetual KYC replaces periodic refresh cycles; full regulatory audit trail is machine-readable and available for SAMA examination on demand; FATF Travel Rule compliance for virtual asset-related transactions

subAreas.name: Account Opening
subAreas.description: Customer account creation, Islamic or conventional product selection, documentation, and activation workflow
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about the end-to-end account opening process from customer application to account activation — how long it takes, how many manual handoffs exist, what systems are involved (CRM, core banking, document management), how incomplete applications are tracked and followed up, what the rejection rate is and what are the common reasons, how deduplication of existing customers is handled using SIMAH or internal customer master, what Shari'ah-compliant account products are offered (Wadiah, Amanah) and how product selection is guided, and what the first-funding and first-transaction activation rates look like.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Account opening is a multi-day, branch-only process requiring physical presence and paper forms; data is manually re-keyed into the core banking system; deduplication relies on branch staff memory; Shari'ah product selection guidance is informal; average TAT is 3-5 working days; no integration with Nafath or Absher
benchmarks.maturity_2: Digital application form captures customer data but still requires branch visit for document submission and signature; core banking entry is semi-automated from the form data; basic deduplication checks run against the internal customer master; Nafath ID verification is available at the branch terminal; average TAT is 1-2 working days
benchmarks.maturity_3: Fully digital account opening journey for individual savings and current accounts; Nafath-based e-KYC eliminates branch visit for eligible Saudi nationals and Iqama-holding residents; application data flows automatically into the core banking system with configurable approval workflows; deduplication runs against SIMAH and internal customer master; TAT is same-day for straight-through cases; exception queue managed by a dedicated operations team; Shari'ah product recommendations are embedded in the digital journey
benchmarks.maturity_4: Omnichannel account opening with consistent experience across mobile app, internet banking, and assisted channels; AI-powered pre-fill using SIMAH data and SAMA Open Banking data with customer consent; real-time decisioning on account type eligibility and Islamic product bundling recommendations; STP rate >80%; average TAT under 15 minutes for digital-native Saudi national customers; PDPL-compliant consent capture is embedded in the flow
benchmarks.maturity_5: Hyper-personalised account opening journey that pre-selects Shari'ah-compliant product bundles based on customer financial profile accessed via SAMA Open Banking consent; zero-touch STP for compliant customer segments; predictive models recommend cross-sell at point of account opening; near-zero TAT for eligible customers; continuous journey analytics identify drop-off points and trigger recovery flows; full PDPL audit trail from consent to data usage

subAreas.name: Digital Onboarding
subAreas.description: Post-account digital channel activation, mobile and internet banking enrolment, mada card issuance, and early engagement
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about what happens after an account is opened — how customers are enrolled in internet and mobile banking, how mada debit card issuance and activation are handled, how digital payment wallet linkage (stc pay, Apple Pay, STC Pay) is facilitated, what the digital activation rate is (percentage of newly opened accounts that transact digitally within 30 days), how the bank measures and improves early engagement, what onboarding communication journeys look like via SMS and WhatsApp, and how drop-offs in activation are identified and recovered.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Digital channel enrolment requires a separate branch visit or mailed PIN; mada debit card is dispatched by post with 7-14 day lead time; no structured post-onboarding communication; digital activation rate is below 20%; no visibility into where customers drop off in the activation funnel
benchmarks.maturity_2: Internet banking credentials are issued via email/SMS at account opening; mobile app download link is shared; mada card registration and digital wallet linkage require manual steps; mada card is couriered within 3-5 days; basic welcome SMS/email sequence exists; digital activation rate 20-40%
benchmarks.maturity_3: Integrated digital onboarding journey activates internet banking, mobile app, and mada card at account opening; virtual mada card is issued instantly for digital transactions; Apple Pay and stc pay tokenisation is offered at account opening; welcome journey includes triggered SMS/WhatsApp milestones; digital activation rate 40-60%; dashboards track activation funnel by segment and channel
benchmarks.maturity_4: One-click digital channel activation embedded in the account opening flow with Nafath-verified authentication; instant virtual mada card with SAMA-compliant spending controls; AI-driven personalised onboarding journey selects communication content and timing based on customer profile; proactive outreach to at-risk non-activating customers via WhatsApp and push notification; digital activation rate 60-80%; ARPU uplift tracked per onboarding cohort
benchmarks.maturity_5: Zero-friction digital-first onboarding where all channel activations complete during account opening via Nafath; predictive next-best-action engine guides customers to their first meaningful transaction within 24 hours; real-time A/B testing of onboarding journeys; digital activation rate >80%; full attribution of onboarding journey to LTV outcomes; SAMA Consumer Protection Guidelines on digital onboarding are embedded in the journey design

broadAreas.name: Financing Origination
broadAreas.description: End-to-end Islamic and conventional financing lifecycle from application through credit assessment, underwriting, sanction, and disbursement

subAreas.name: Credit Assessment
subAreas.description: Creditworthiness evaluation, SIMAH bureau pull, financial analysis, risk rating, and Shari'ah compliance verification
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how credit decisions are made — what bureau data is pulled from SIMAH (Saudi Credit Bureau), how financial statements are analysed for corporate and SME financing, what internal credit scoring models exist, how risk ratings are assigned, what the average cycle time is from application to credit decision, how the credit committee process works for large exposures, what override rates look like and how they are controlled, how the bank uses ZATCA data and SAMA Open Banking data (with customer consent) as alternative data sources for thin-file customers, and how the credit assessment process handles Islamic financing products (Murabaha, Ijarah, Musharakah) differently from conventional credit.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Credit assessment is fully manual; financing officers pull SIMAH reports individually and perform financial analysis in Excel; risk ratings are judgement-based with no standardised scorecard; credit committee meetings are scheduled ad-hoc; cycle time from application to decision is 7-15 days; no tracking of override rates; Shari'ah compliance is verified informally
benchmarks.maturity_2: SIMAH integration provides automated credit report pull at application; basic scorecards exist for retail segments but are not integrated into the workflow; financial spreading for business financing is still manual; credit committee is structured with defined thresholds for delegation of authority; decision cycle time is 5-10 days; Shari'ah screening is a separate step
benchmarks.maturity_3: Financing origination system (FOS) integrates SIMAH pull, scorecard execution, and financial spreading in a single workflow; risk ratings are system-generated with manual override capability; delegation of authority matrix is enforced in the system; Shari'ah compliance checks are integrated as a workflow step; decision cycle time is 2-5 days for retail; override rate is tracked and reported to credit risk committee; ZATCA-verified income data is used for salaried applicants
benchmarks.maturity_4: AI-assisted credit models incorporate alternative data (ZATCA VAT returns for SMEs, bank statement analytics via SAMA Open Banking, payroll data via GOSI verification) alongside SIMAH scores; real-time decisioning for pre-approved retail Murabaha products; digital credit memo auto-populates from FOS data for committee review; cycle time <24 hours for retail; override rates are monitored with root cause analysis; Shari'ah screening is automated for standard product structures
benchmarks.maturity_5: Straight-through credit decisioning for eligible retail segments using ML ensemble models with explainability outputs compliant with SAMA model risk guidelines; continuous model retraining using outcome data; SAMA Open Banking and ZATCA data are primary inputs for thin-file customers and SMEs; Shari'ah board digital approval is integrated for complex structures; credit committee is reserved for exceptions and large structured deals; cycle time <1 hour for retail STP cases; full model governance documentation meets SAMA supervisory expectations

subAreas.name: Underwriting & Sanction
subAreas.description: Financing structuring, Islamic product terms definition, collateral valuation, legal due diligence, and sanction letter issuance
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about the underwriting process after credit approval — how financing terms and conditions are structured for Islamic products (Murabaha cost-plus structure, Ijarah lease terms, Diminishing Musharakah ratios), how collateral is valued and registered with the Saudi Real Estate General Authority (REGA) or Ejar platform for leased properties, how legal due diligence is conducted (title deed verification via REGA, Wathiq notarisation), how sanction letters are generated and communicated, how long the sanction-to-disbursement window typically is, and what the main causes of delay in this stage are.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Financing terms are manually drafted in Word documents by financing officers; collateral valuation is paper-based with no panel management; legal due diligence involves physical document scrutiny and manual REGA title searches; sanction letters are typed and physically signed; average sanction-to-disbursement TAT is 15-30 days; Shari'ah contract documentation is prepared manually
benchmarks.maturity_2: Sanction letter templates exist in the FOS but require manual data entry; collateral management module tracks basic security details; empanelled valuation and legal firms are listed but assignment is manual; REGA title search is done via the online portal but not integrated into the workflow; TAT is 10-20 days with manual follow-ups; Wathiq notarisation is handled separately
benchmarks.maturity_3: FOS auto-generates sanction letters and Islamic financing contracts (Murabaha, Ijarah) from approved credit terms; collateral management integrates with property valuation reports uploaded by empanelled valuers via a portal; REGA title deed verification is system-initiated via API; legal due diligence checklist is tracked in the FOS with configurable step approvals; Wathiq notarisation is trackable in the workflow; TAT is 5-10 days for standard cases
benchmarks.maturity_4: Digital sanction letter and Islamic contract documentation with Nafath-based e-signature; automated valuer assignment based on geography and financing type with SLA tracking; API integration with REGA for real-time title verification and lien registration; Wathiq notarisation is digitally initiated; legal due diligence is workflow-managed with automated reminders; TAT is 2-5 days; Shari'ah board digital stamp for approved contract templates
benchmarks.maturity_5: End-to-end digital sanction with zero paper; AI-assisted Islamic financing term structuring suggests profit rates, Ijarah terms, and collateral requirements based on risk profile; automated collateral registration with REGA and relevant Saudi registries; smart legal document review flags anomalies in title deeds and contract terms; TAT <48 hours for secured retail financing products; digital audit trail meets SAMA and Shari'ah audit requirements

subAreas.name: Disbursement
subAreas.description: Financing account creation, funds transfer via SARIE or mada, Islamic contract execution, and post-disbursement documentation
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about the disbursement process — how financing accounts are created in the core banking system, how funds are transferred to customers or directly to real estate developers or car dealers (for Diminishing Musharakah home financing or Murabaha auto financing), how Islamic contract conditions precedent are checked before fund release, what the profit payment and repayment schedule setup looks like, how disbursement errors or reversals are handled, and what the disbursement STP rate is across different financing products (Murabaha, Ijarah, Musharakah).

benchmarks.maturity_1: Financing account creation is manual in core banking; disbursement cheques are physically prepared; contract conditions precedent are checked from a paper checklist; repayment schedule is communicated via post; errors require manual reversal; no STP; TAT from sanction to disbursement is 5-10 days; Murabaha asset purchase and resale steps are handled manually
benchmarks.maturity_2: Financing account is created in core banking from FOS data with some manual re-keying; disbursement is via SARIE/mada transfer initiated manually by operations team; contract conditions are tracked in a spreadsheet; profit schedule is system-generated but communicated via email; Murabaha and Ijarah disbursement steps are tracked separately; STP rate <20%
benchmarks.maturity_3: FOS-to-core banking integration auto-creates financing account on sanction confirmation; disbursement is initiated from FOS via SARIE payment gateway with four-eye check; contract condition tracking is in-system with document upload by branch or customer; profit schedule and welcome letter are auto-generated in Arabic and English; Murabaha asset purchase confirmation triggers fund release automatically; STP rate 40-60%
benchmarks.maturity_4: Straight-through disbursement for conditions-met cases without manual intervention; direct disbursement to real estate developer or car dealer accounts for home and auto financing via SARIE API integration; customer receives real-time WhatsApp/SMS notification with digital financing agreement and repayment schedule; exceptions are routed to an operations queue; STP rate 60-80%; disbursement TAT <24 hours for STP cases; multi-tranche disbursement for construction-linked Istisna financing is orchestrated automatically
benchmarks.maturity_5: Fully automated disbursement engine with zero manual touchpoints for compliant cases; AI validates contract conditions precedent from uploaded documents; multi-party disbursement orchestration for complex Islamic structures (construction-linked Istisna, syndicated Musharakah); real-time reconciliation between FOS, core banking, and SARIE payment system; STP rate >80%; end-to-end disbursement audit log meets SAMA and Shari'ah supervisory requirements

broadAreas.name: Payments & Settlement
broadAreas.description: Processing of domestic and cross-border payment transactions, interbank settlement via SARIE, and reconciliation

subAreas.name: SARIE / mada / SADAD Processing
subAreas.description: Domestic payment channel operations covering SARIE (RTGS), mada (debit network), SADAD (bill payments), and digital wallets
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank processes domestic payments across channels (SARIE for high-value RTGS, mada for debit transactions, SADAD for bill payments, stc pay and Apple Pay for digital wallets) — what payment hubs or systems are in place, how transaction routing works, how SARIE cut-off windows are managed, what the failure and return rates are by channel, how failed transactions are investigated and resolved, what the Direct Debit mandate management process looks like under Saudi Payments rules, and how the bank handles high-volume peak periods (government salary cycles via GOSI, Ramadan/Hajj season spikes, quarter-end).

benchmarks.maturity_1: Payment processing is siloed by channel with separate systems for SARIE and mada; manual intervention is required for failed transactions; SARIE cut-off management is tracked in spreadsheets; SADAD bill payment integration is basic; no real-time visibility into transaction status; failure resolution relies on branch staff calling operations; Direct Debit mandates are processed via paper forms
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic payment hub aggregates SARIE and mada channels; SADAD is on a separate stack; failed transaction queues exist but resolution is manual; Direct Debit mandates are processed electronically via Saudi Payments but mandate management is in a separate system; management reports on channel volumes are generated daily
benchmarks.maturity_3: Unified payment hub processes SARIE, mada, and SADAD with centralised routing and cut-off management; failed transaction alerts are system-generated with auto-classification by failure reason; Direct Debit mandate creation, amendment, and cancellation are end-to-end digital; real-time dashboards show channel health and transaction volumes; API-based payment status enquiry for corporate customers; ISO 20022 messaging is supported for SARIE transactions
benchmarks.maturity_4: Intelligent payment routing selects optimal channel based on amount, urgency, and cost; predictive failure detection flags transactions at risk before SARIE submission; auto-remediation resolves >60% of failed transactions without human intervention; Direct Debit delinquency prediction enables proactive outreach before mandate failures; real-time reconciliation against Saudi Payments settlement files; cross-border payments via SWIFT gpi and Arab Monetary Fund's Buna platform are integrated
benchmarks.maturity_5: AI-orchestrated payment engine self-tunes routing rules based on real-time channel performance across SARIE, mada, and SADAD; near-zero manual intervention for transaction exceptions; fraud detection is embedded in the payment flow with sub-second decisioning compliant with SAMA fraud risk guidelines; full ISO 20022 rich data payment capability; cross-border GCC payment corridors integrated via Buna and SWIFT gpi; continuous SLA monitoring with automated SAMA regulatory reporting on payment system performance

subAreas.name: Reconciliation
subAreas.description: Interbank, internal, and customer-level reconciliation of SARIE, mada, and SADAD transactions against GL entries
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank reconciles payment transactions — how entries in the core banking system are matched against Saudi Payments and SARIE settlement files, how internal inter-branch and inter-system entries are reconciled, how long outstanding items sit before escalation, what the volume of open reconciliation items is, how automated the matching process is (STP rate), who owns the reconciliation process and what the governance structure looks like, and what happens when reconciliation breaks at month-end or during peak periods like Eid or Hajj season.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Reconciliation is entirely manual using Excel; staff download SARIE and mada settlement files and match line by line against core banking exports; outstanding items are tracked in spreadsheets; month-end breaks are resolved reactively; dedicated reconciliation team is understaffed and relies on overtime at cut-off periods; no audit trail on matching decisions
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic reconciliation tool performs automated matching for high-volume routine items; complex or exception items still require manual research; outstanding item aging reports are generated weekly; inter-branch entries are reconciled monthly; month-end reconciliation takes 3-5 days
benchmarks.maturity_3: Automated reconciliation platform matches transactions across SARIE, mada, SADAD, and internal GL feeds with configurable matching rules; unmatched items are auto-classified and routed to the appropriate team; aging thresholds trigger escalation alerts; inter-system entries are reconciled daily; month-end close supported by a centralised breaks register; STP matching rate 60-75%
benchmarks.maturity_4: AI-powered matching engine applies fuzzy logic and ML to resolve near-matches reducing manual review by >50%; real-time reconciliation for mada and SADAD settlements; automated journal posting for matched items; dashboards provide live visibility of reconciliation health by channel and entity; STP matching rate 75-90%; month-end close completed within 1 business day; peak season capacity scales automatically
benchmarks.maturity_5: Continuous reconciliation with zero batch dependency; ML models predict and pre-resolve likely breaks before settlement files arrive; full GL lockdown post-close with automated variance analysis; STP matching rate >90%; reconciliation is a zero-touch process for >95% of transaction volume; all reconciliation data is available for SAMA supervisory review on demand

subAreas.name: Nostro & Vostro Management
subAreas.description: Foreign currency account management, GCC correspondent banking, SWIFT message processing, and cross-border payment via Buna
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank manages its nostro accounts with correspondent banks — how many nostro accounts exist across how many currencies and correspondents (including major GCC, USD, EUR, GBP accounts), how daily nostro positions are tracked and reconciled against SWIFT MT940/MT950 statements, how the bank manages forex funding and overdraft risk in nostro accounts, what the process is for investigating SWIFT message exceptions and gpi status enquiries, how SWIFT connectivity is maintained (service bureau or direct), how the bank participates in the Arab Monetary Fund's Buna cross-border payment platform, and how correspondent banking relationships are managed in the context of de-risking trends affecting MENA banks.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Nostro positions are tracked manually in Excel with data pulled from SWIFT MT940 statements by hand; reconciliation with internal Vostro entries is done weekly; overdraft monitoring relies on treasury staff checking balances; SWIFT message exceptions are investigated by emailing correspondent banks; no participation in Buna platform; no centralised nostro management system
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic treasury management system (TMS) imports SWIFT statements and tracks nostro balances; daily reconciliation is partially automated for major currencies (USD, EUR, GBP); SWIFT connectivity is via a service bureau; exception investigation is managed via a shared mailbox; correspondent bank SLAs are informally tracked; Buna participation is under evaluation
benchmarks.maturity_3: TMS provides real-time nostro position visibility across all currencies and GCC correspondents; automated SWIFT statement reconciliation with daily exception reports; intraday liquidity monitoring for major nostro accounts; SWIFT gpi tracker is integrated for outbound payment status; Buna platform is live for select GCC payment corridors; correspondent bank relationships are reviewed quarterly with performance dashboards
benchmarks.maturity_4: Predictive nostro funding engine optimises cross-currency positions to minimise idle balances and overdraft costs; automated SWIFT gpi status updates are surfaced to corporate clients; AI-assisted anomaly detection flags unusual nostro movements for investigation; Buna and SWIFT gpi are used for all eligible cross-border payments; regulatory reporting on correspondent banking is auto-generated; de-risking exposure is monitored with proactive correspondent bank diversification across GCC and global corridors
benchmarks.maturity_5: Real-time multi-currency nostro optimisation using ML forecasting of GCC and global payment flows; direct SWIFT connectivity with full ISO 20022 message processing; Buna integration delivers instant GCC cross-border settlement eliminating overnight exposure for intra-GCC corridors; full intraday liquidity stress testing is automated; regulatory and board reporting on correspondent risk is zero-touch; SAMA reporting on cross-border flows is automated

broadAreas.name: Collections & Recovery
broadAreas.description: Delinquency management, NPL classification under SAMA prudential standards, recovery actions, and Islamic finance restructuring

subAreas.name: Early Delinquency Management
subAreas.description: Pre-delinquency and early bucket (0-90 DPD) collections, customer contact, and Islamic finance restructuring options
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank manages early-stage delinquency — what triggers a collection action (days past due thresholds), how customers are contacted (IVR, SMS, WhatsApp, field agent, branch), how the bank prioritises which accounts to contact first, what restructuring and resolution options are available at this stage (including Islamic restructuring such as Murabaha rescheduling and Tawarruq-based cash relief), how collection agent performance is tracked, what the roll rate from early bucket to NPL is, and how the bank uses predictive analytics to identify at-risk customers before they become delinquent — particularly GOSI payroll data signals and SIMAH early warning indicators.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Collections activities begin only after significant delinquency; customer contact relies on branch staff making ad-hoc phone calls; no centralised collections system; Islamic restructuring requests are handled case by case with no standard Shari'ah-approved process; roll rate from early bucket to NPL is not tracked; no predictive capability
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic collections management system triggers SMS reminders at defined DPD thresholds; field collection agents are deployed for higher-value accounts; agent activity is logged in spreadsheets; standard Shari'ah-approved restructuring products (profit holiday, tenor extension under Murabaha rescheduling) exist but approval is manual; roll rate is monitored monthly
benchmarks.maturity_3: Centralised collections platform manages contact strategy across IVR, SMS, WhatsApp, and field agents based on DPD bucket, product, and customer segment; agent performance is tracked in real-time; digital self-service portal allows customers to raise restructuring requests; SIMAH early warning signals are used to identify deteriorating accounts; roll rate by product and vintage is tracked weekly; early warning system flags accounts 30 days before first missed payment based on GOSI payroll data and payment behaviour
benchmarks.maturity_4: AI-driven contact strategy optimiser selects channel, timing, and message content based on predicted response propensity; predictive delinquency models identify at-risk customers 60-90 days before first DPD enabling proactive Shari'ah-compliant restructuring offers; automated restructuring approval for standard Murabaha rescheduling cases within defined risk parameters; roll rate improvement tracked as a primary collections KPI; digital-first self-cure options embedded in mobile banking
benchmarks.maturity_5: Real-time collections orchestration engine adapts strategy dynamically based on customer response signals; ML models continuously learn from resolution outcomes to improve contact and offer strategies; digital-first collections with human agents reserved for complex or high-value cases; self-cure rate >40% through automated digital interventions including Tawarruq-based liquidity solutions; end-to-end collections lifecycle is zero-touch for STP-eligible resolutions; SAMA Consumer Protection Guidelines on debt collection are embedded in all contact strategies

subAreas.name: NPL Classification & SAMA Prudential Standards
subAreas.description: Non-Performing Loan identification, SAMA-compliant classification, IFRS 9 provisioning, and restructured financing management
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank identifies and classifies Non-Performing Loans (NPLs) — how the 90-DPD rule is applied and monitored in line with SAMA prudential guidelines, how the NPL classification process works in the core banking system, how IFRS 9 Expected Credit Loss (ECL) provisioning is calculated across Stage 1, 2, and 3 exposures, how restructured Islamic financing facilities are tracked under SAMA's guidelines on Shari'ah-compliant restructuring, what the gross and net NPL ratios are, how the bank manages its provision coverage ratio target, and how NPL data flows into SAMA supervisory submissions.

benchmarks.maturity_1: NPL identification relies on manual review of overdue accounts by branch staff; classification is done retrospectively and inaccurately; IFRS 9 ECL calculations are performed in Excel by the finance team; SAMA supervisory submissions on asset quality are manual and often delayed; gross NPL ratio is not tracked at portfolio level; no early warning system; Shari'ah-compliant restructuring classification rules are not systematically applied
benchmarks.maturity_2: Core banking system automatically flags accounts at 90 DPD; classification is system-driven for retail but manual review is required for corporate accounts; ECL provisioning is calculated by the finance team using system-generated aging reports; SAMA asset quality data is extracted from core banking and submitted via the SAMA supervisory portal; gross NPL ratio is reported monthly
benchmarks.maturity_3: Automated NPL classification engine in core banking applies SAMA's IRAC-equivalent norms across all products and segments including Islamic financing; IFRS 9 ECL is calculated and posted automatically at month-end with exception review; restructured financing tracking is fully integrated with Shari'ah-compliant classification rules; SAMA asset quality supervisory submissions are generated from system data with maker-checker controls; NPL ratio is tracked weekly with drill-down by product, geography, and vintage
benchmarks.maturity_4: Real-time NPL monitoring with intraday classification updates; AI-assisted income recognition and ECL provisioning for complex Islamic exposures (Musharakah, Ijarah); SAMA supervisory submissions are automated end-to-end with internal validation rules; NPL ratio forecasting uses financing-level roll rate models; early warning signals (EWS) using SIMAH data, GOSI payroll signals, and ZATCA business data are integrated with collections for pre-NPL intervention; board-level NPL dashboard is auto-refreshed
benchmarks.maturity_5: Continuous NPL lifecycle management with zero batch latency; ML models predict NPL formation 90-180 days in advance with account-level probability scores; IFRS 9 ECL is dynamically adjusted using forward-looking macro scenarios calibrated to Saudi economic indicators; SAMA API submissions are real-time where SAMA infrastructure supports it; full credit risk data fabric integrates NPL management with capital planning and ICAAP stress testing under SAMA guidelines

subAreas.name: Legal Recovery & Saudi Enforcement Framework
subAreas.description: Legal proceedings under Saudi Bankruptcy Law and Real Estate Financing Law, Enforcement Court actions, OTS management, and write-off management
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank handles legal recovery for severely delinquent accounts — when and how formal demand notices are issued under Saudi contract law, how cases are filed with the Saudi Commercial Court or Enforcement Court, how the bank manages its empanelled legal counsels and tracks case progress, what the process is for enforcing collateral rights (property auction under REGA, vehicle repossession), how Shari'ah-compliant One-Time Settlement (sulh) proposals are evaluated and approved, what the write-off policy is and how technical write-offs are managed, and how recovery from written-off accounts is tracked.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Legal recovery actions are initiated reactively and inconsistently; demand notices are drafted manually by the legal team; case tracking is in spreadsheets; empanelled counsel performance is not monitored; property auction process under REGA is ad-hoc; OTS approvals are undocumented; write-offs require board approval regardless of size; recovery from written-off accounts is not tracked
benchmarks.maturity_2: Legal case management module tracks Commercial Court and Enforcement Court actions by account; standard notice templates reduce drafting effort; counsel assignment is tracked centrally; property valuation for auction is conducted by REGA-certified valuers; Shari'ah-compliant OTS framework with defined eligibility and discount authority exists; write-off policy is documented with defined authority levels
benchmarks.maturity_3: Integrated legal recovery system manages the full enforcement lifecycle (demand notice, court filing, asset attachment, auction via REGA) with automated milestone tracking; Saudi Commercial Court and Enforcement Court case management is workflow-managed with hearing date tracking; counsel performance scorecards are maintained; e-auction via REGA-approved platforms is used for collateral disposal; OTS (sulh) proposals are evaluated against a standard Shari'ah-compliant financial model; recovery from written-off accounts is tracked in a dedicated module
benchmarks.maturity_4: AI-assisted triage determines the optimal recovery strategy (Enforcement Court vs settlement vs compromise) based on account characteristics and recovery probability models; automated demand notice generation with digital delivery tracking; collateral auction via REGA e-auction platform with real-time bidding; OTS decisioning is rule-based for standard cases under Shari'ah board-approved parameters; recovery rate by legal strategy is analysed to optimise strategy selection; Saudi Bankruptcy Law proceedings are workflow-managed for complex corporate cases
benchmarks.maturity_5: End-to-end digital legal recovery engine with zero-touch case initiation for eligible accounts; ML models predict recovery timelines and amounts by strategy enabling portfolio-level recovery planning; REGA-integrated property title transfer post-auction; SAMA reporting on NPL recovery is automated; OTS acceptance probability scoring enables pre-emptive Shari'ah-compliant negotiation; recovery from written-off pools is managed as a distinct asset class with dedicated portfolio analytics under Saudi Bankruptcy Law framework

broadAreas.name: Regulatory Reporting
broadAreas.description: SAMA supervisory returns, capital adequacy reporting, statutory filings, PDPL compliance, and regulatory data management

subAreas.name: SAMA Supervisory Returns & Regulatory Submissions
subAreas.description: Periodic statutory returns to SAMA including prudential returns, credit risk data, liquidity reports, and consumer finance disclosures
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about the bank's process for preparing and submitting SAMA regulatory returns — what returns are submitted (monthly prudential returns, quarterly capital adequacy returns, annual ICAAP, credit risk classification reports, consumer finance reports, AML/CFT annual reports to SAFIU, etc.), how data is extracted and validated for these submissions, what the timeline and governance process is, how errors or resubmissions are handled, what the main sources of data quality issues are, how the bank manages the increasing burden of regulatory reporting under SAMA's expanding supervisory framework, and whether any returns have attracted SAMA observations or penalties.

benchmarks.maturity_1: SAMA returns are prepared manually by the compliance team using data extracted in spreadsheets from multiple source systems; each return requires significant manual data collection and reconciliation; submissions are made close to or past SAMA deadlines; data quality issues are discovered post-submission; no centralised regulatory data repository; SAMA observations or penalties for late or incorrect submissions have been received
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic regulatory reporting module extracts data from core banking for major SAMA returns; manual validation checklists exist; a compliance calendar tracks submission deadlines; most returns are submitted on time; resubmissions are managed via the SAMA supervisory portal; data quality is inconsistent for non-core data elements
benchmarks.maturity_3: Centralised regulatory reporting platform aggregates data from core banking, FOS, and payment systems for all major SAMA returns; automated validation rules flag data quality issues before submission; submission tracking dashboard monitors status by return type; maker-checker workflow enforces governance; submission history with version control is maintained; resubmissions are tracked and root-caused; SAMA circular changes are managed through a regulatory change process
benchmarks.maturity_4: Regulatory data mart with a single golden source for all SAMA-required data elements; automated end-to-end return preparation with exception-only human review; submission SLA compliance is >99%; regulatory change management process updates reporting rules within 30 days of SAMA circulars; AI-assisted anomaly detection flags potential errors before submission; SAMA feedback from inspections is tracked and closed within defined timelines; PDPL data handling obligations are reflected in reporting data flows
benchmarks.maturity_5: Real-time regulatory data fabric enables on-demand generation of any SAMA return from a single dataset; API-based direct submission to SAMA supervisory portals eliminating manual upload; continuous compliance monitoring identifies regulatory data gaps as they occur; regulatory change is auto-parsed from SAMA circulars using NLP and mapped to data requirements; zero submission failures; full regulatory data lineage from source to submission; SAMA examination data requests are fulfilled within hours

subAreas.name: Basel III / Capital Adequacy
subAreas.description: Capital ratio computation, ICAAP, stress testing, and Pillar 3 disclosures under SAMA's Basel III implementation framework
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank computes and manages its capital adequacy under SAMA's Basel III framework — how CET1, Tier 1, and Total Capital ratios are calculated, what systems are used for RWA computation, how the bank manages its capital buffer above the SAMA regulatory minimum (and any SAMA-imposed Pillar 2 add-ons), what the ICAAP process looks like, how stress testing is conducted using SAMA-prescribed scenarios, how capital planning is integrated with business planning and Vision 2030 growth objectives, and how Pillar 3 disclosures are prepared and published on the bank's investor relations site.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Capital adequacy computation is done manually in Excel using data extracted from core banking; RWA calculations are basic and may not capture all risk exposures accurately; ICAAP is a compliance exercise with limited analytical depth; stress testing is rudimentary; Pillar 3 disclosures are prepared by the compliance team with significant effort; capital buffer management is reactive with limited SAMA buffer guidance tracking
benchmarks.maturity_2: Dedicated capital management module computes standard approach RWA for credit, market, and operational risk; quarterly capital ratios are reviewed by the ALCO; ICAAP documentation follows SAMA guidance; basic stress scenarios (credit shock, oil price shock affecting Saudi economy) are modelled annually; Pillar 3 disclosures are published semi-annually on the bank's investor relations website
benchmarks.maturity_3: Integrated capital management platform computes RWA daily from transaction-level data; ICAAP is a comprehensive analytical exercise reviewed by the board risk committee incorporating Saudi macro-economic scenarios; stress testing covers a range of scenarios including oil price shocks and real estate market stress; capital planning is linked to the annual budgeting process and FSDP growth targets; Pillar 3 disclosures are published quarterly; capital ratio is monitored against internal buffers with breach alerts
benchmarks.maturity_4: Real-time capital ratio monitoring with intraday RWA updates for material transactions; SAMA-approved IRB approaches for credit RWA reduce capital requirements for qualifying portfolios; ICAAP uses a dynamic capital model with reverse stress testing calibrated to Saudi macro scenarios; scenario analysis supports strategic decisions aligned with Vision 2030; Pillar 3 disclosure preparation is largely automated; capital optimisation analytics identify RWA-dense portfolios including Islamic financing structures
benchmarks.maturity_5: Continuous capital optimisation engine integrates capital planning, pricing, and portfolio management; ML-assisted credit RWA computation improves accuracy for complex Islamic exposures; real-time stress testing is embedded in business decision workflows; automated Pillar 3 XBRL-format disclosure to SAMA and investor portals; full capital data lineage from transaction to ratio; capital and liquidity risk management are fully integrated in a common risk data architecture; SAMA ICAAP submission is auto-generated and board-approved digitally

subAreas.name: SAMA Compliance & Regulatory Registrations
subAreas.description: Collateral registration with Saudi Real Estate General Authority (REGA), SIMAH credit data submissions, PDPL compliance, and other statutory registry obligations
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank manages its statutory registration and compliance obligations — how collateral security interests are registered with the Saudi Real Estate General Authority (REGA) at financing disbursement and released at closure, how credit data is submitted to SIMAH (Saudi Credit Bureau) on a timely and accurate basis, how the bank ensures compliance with the Saudi Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) including data subject rights, consent management, and breach reporting to SDAIA, how Wathiq document notarisation obligations are fulfilled, and whether the bank has faced any SAMA, REGA, SIMAH, or SDAIA audit observations.

benchmarks.maturity_1: REGA collateral registration is done manually by operations staff after receiving a paper-based request from the branch; delays of 30+ days between disbursement and REGA registration are common; SIMAH data submissions are similarly manual and delayed; PDPL obligations are not systematically tracked; registration failures are discovered only during SAMA inspection; no tracking of outstanding or failed registrations; Wathiq notarisation is untracked
benchmarks.maturity_2: REGA registration is initiated from the core banking system but requires manual data entry; a daily exception report tracks overdue registrations; SIMAH submissions are done in weekly batches; registration status is tracked in a register; PDPL policy exists but implementation is inconsistent; SAMA audit observations have been received on registration delays and data quality
benchmarks.maturity_3: FOS triggers automated REGA registration API call on disbursement confirmation; registration success or failure is surfaced in the FOS exception queue; SIMAH data submission is integrated into the account opening and financing workflows with same-day upload; registration release on financing closure is system-initiated; a dashboard tracks REGA registration SLA compliance by branch and product; PDPL consent management is embedded in customer onboarding with automated data retention enforcement
benchmarks.maturity_4: Zero-delay REGA registration with real-time API integration at disbursement; automated retry logic handles API failures with escalation alerts; SIMAH records are updated in real-time on customer data changes and financing events; PDPL data subject rights requests (access, deletion, correction) are managed in a dedicated workflow with regulatory timelines enforced; REGA registration error rate is tracked as a primary compliance KPI; SDAIA breach notification workflow is tested and documented
benchmarks.maturity_5: REGA registration and SIMAH submissions are zero-touch automated processes with sub-minute completion at disbursement; ML-based pre-validation eliminates registration failures before submission; PDPL compliance is continuously monitored with automated data lineage tracking from collection to deletion; full audit trail of all REGA, SIMAH, and SDAIA interactions is immutable; regulatory change in REGA or SIMAH data requirements is auto-mapped and deployed; compliance with all statutory registry obligations is continuous and zero-effort; NCA cybersecurity certification requirements are integrated into the operational risk framework

broadAreas.name: Core Banking Operations
broadAreas.description: Day-to-day banking operations including batch processing, liquidity management under SAMA standards, and fraud risk oversight

subAreas.name: EOD / BOD Batch Processing
subAreas.description: End-of-day and beginning-of-day core banking batch cycles on Temenos T24, FLEXCUBE, or iMAL, profit accrual, and operational continuity
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about the bank's core banking batch processing on their platform (Temenos T24, Oracle FLEXCUBE, Path Solutions iMAL, or equivalent) — how long the EOD batch takes, what the most frequent causes of batch failures or delays are, how Islamic profit accrual and capitalisation are handled (including distinction between profit receivable on Murabaha and rental income on Ijarah), how the bank manages EOD completion monitoring, what the escalation process is for batch failures, how BCP is exercised for core banking system outages aligned with SAMA Business Continuity guidelines, and how the bank is modernising its batch architecture.

benchmarks.maturity_1: EOD batch takes 6-8 hours and frequently overruns into business hours; batch failures are investigated by IT team with no standard runbook; Islamic profit accrual errors are discovered post-EOD; monitoring is manual with staff watching batch logs; BCP for core banking has not been tested in the last 12 months; no visibility into batch step SLAs; SAMA BCP regulatory requirements are not fully met
benchmarks.maturity_2: EOD batch completes in 3-6 hours on most nights; standard batch monitoring tool alerts on step failures; runbooks exist for common failures; profit accrual is validated by a post-batch exception report; BCP is tested annually in line with SAMA guidelines; batch SLAs are informally tracked
benchmarks.maturity_3: EOD batch is optimised to run in <3 hours with parallel processing of independent batch jobs; automated monitoring platform tracks step-level SLAs and pages the on-call team on breach; profit accrual validation (Murabaha, Ijarah, Musharakah) is automated with auto-correction of minor discrepancies; BOD processes (Hijri/Gregorian date change, profit rate updates) are scripted and verified; BCP is tested semi-annually with documented RTO meeting SAMA BCP standards; batch completion report is distributed automatically
benchmarks.maturity_4: Continuous batch monitoring with predictive SLA breach detection based on job run-time trends; automated remediation handles >50% of batch exceptions without human intervention; profit and fee calculations are validated in real-time during the batch using control totals; BCP failover has been tested with live traffic; event-driven processing is partially implemented reducing batch dependency for select processes; batch window is <2 hours; Hijri calendar-specific processing (Ramadan, Hajj schedules) is automated
benchmarks.maturity_5: Core banking is on a near-real-time processing architecture with minimal batch dependency; remaining batch jobs are fully automated and self-healing; zero unplanned EOD overruns in the past 12 months; BCP failover is automatic with <15-minute RTO exceeding SAMA BCP requirements; Islamic profit accrual is a continuous event-driven calculation; core banking platform supports cloud-native elastic scaling for peak period processing; NCA-compliant cybersecurity controls are embedded in core banking operations

subAreas.name: Liquidity Management & ALM
subAreas.description: Intraday and structural liquidity monitoring, asset-liability management, Islamic profit rate risk, and SAMA liquidity compliance
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank manages its liquidity position — how the LCR (Liquidity Coverage Ratio) and NSFR are monitored and reported to SAMA, how the ALCO reviews and manages the balance sheet including Islamic finance-specific ALM considerations, what tools are used for Profit Rate Risk (PRR) measurement for Islamic banks (equivalent to interest rate risk — NII sensitivity and Economic Value of Equity), how the bank manages its SAMA statutory deposit requirements, how intraday liquidity is monitored across SARIE positions and internal systems, and how the bank's liquidity stress testing framework incorporates Saudi-specific scenarios (oil price shock, real estate market stress, seasonal outflows during Hajj and Ramadan).

benchmarks.maturity_1: LCR and NSFR calculations are done manually in Excel using data extracted from core banking and treasury systems; ALCO meets monthly with backward-looking data; Profit Rate Risk is measured using basic repricing gap analysis in spreadsheets; SAMA statutory deposit compliance is monitored by a single treasury officer; intraday liquidity is not monitored; no Saudi-specific liquidity stress testing framework
benchmarks.maturity_2: Dedicated ALM system calculates LCR, NSFR, and basic PRR metrics; ALCO receives a weekly risk report; SAMA statutory deposit positions are tracked in the treasury system with breach alerts; repricing gap covers a 12-month horizon; basic stress testing scenarios are run quarterly; LCR is reported to SAMA via supervisory returns
benchmarks.maturity_3: Integrated ALM platform calculates LCR, NSFR, NII sensitivity, and EVE daily from enriched data feeds incorporating Islamic finance instrument behaviours; ALCO is supported by an analytics team with scenario-based dashboards; SAMA statutory deposit compliance is real-time with automated alert on breach risk; intraday liquidity is monitored for SARIE payment system positions; liquidity stress testing is conducted quarterly using SAMA-prescribed scenarios including Saudi-specific macro shocks; Pillar 2 liquidity risk assessment is embedded in ICAAP
benchmarks.maturity_4: Real-time LCR and intraday liquidity monitoring via SARIE with automated alert on early warning indicators; predictive cash flow modelling improves HQLA buffer optimisation; ALM analytics incorporate Islamic finance behavioural assumptions for profit payment patterns and prepayment rates; PRR is managed dynamically with hedging and Islamic derivative (Wa'd-based) recommendations from the ALM system; stress testing covers idiosyncratic, Saudi macro, and GCC-wide scenarios; capital and liquidity planning are integrated
benchmarks.maturity_5: Continuous liquidity risk management with real-time data from all transaction systems and SARIE; ML-based behavioural modelling for retail deposits (Wadiah, savings) and financing prepayments improves ALM accuracy; intraday SARIE liquidity is optimised with automated HQLA collateral management; dynamic stress testing is embedded in business decision workflows; LCR and NSFR are always above SAMA internal buffer thresholds with zero manual intervention; regulatory submissions are automated and real-time via SAMA API

subAreas.name: Fraud & Operational Risk Monitoring
subAreas.description: Transaction fraud detection, cybersecurity under NCA framework, operational risk event management, and SAMA fraud reporting
subAreas.basePrompt: Ask about how the bank manages fraud and operational risk — what fraud detection systems are in place for mada card fraud, digital banking fraud, and account takeover fraud, how quickly suspicious transactions are flagged and actioned in compliance with SAMA Consumer Protection guidelines, what the false positive rate is for fraud alerts, how operational risk events (including cyber incidents) are captured and reported to SAMA and to NCA (National Cybersecurity Authority) under the Saudi Cybersecurity Framework, how the bank manages its RCSA (Risk and Control Self-Assessment) process, and how PDPL data breach obligations are met including mandatory notification to SDAIA.

benchmarks.maturity_1: Fraud detection relies on reactive investigation after customer complaints; no automated transaction monitoring for fraud; mada card disputes are managed manually; operational risk events are reported informally; no RCSA process; SAMA fraud reporting is done manually and often delayed; NCA cybersecurity incident reporting obligations are not met consistently; no KRI framework; PDPL breach notification process is undefined
benchmarks.maturity_2: Basic rule-based fraud detection system flags high-risk mada transactions for review; card fraud disputes are tracked in a module; operational risk events are logged in a register; annual RCSA is conducted by business units; SAMA fraud reporting is structured but relies on manual data collection; NCA cybersecurity controls are partially implemented; basic KRIs are reported to the risk committee quarterly
benchmarks.maturity_3: Enterprise fraud management system (FMS) monitors transactions in real-time across mada, digital banking, and SARIE channels with configurable rules and scoring; fraud case management module handles investigation and customer communication in line with SAMA Consumer Protection guidelines; operational risk management system (ORMS) captures risk events, near-misses, and losses; RCSA is conducted semi-annually with control testing; SAMA fraud reporting is automated; NCA cybersecurity framework controls are mapped to the bank's control library; BEICF and KRI dashboards are available to risk management; PDPL breach response playbook is documented
benchmarks.maturity_4: AI/ML-based fraud detection with customer behavioural profiles and network analysis reduces false positives by >30% while improving detection rates; real-time mada card blocking on high-risk transactions; digital fraud (phishing, SIM swap, Absher account hijacking) detection integrated with mobile banking platform; operational risk data is aggregated with industry loss database benchmarking; RCSA is continuous with automated control testing; NCA cybersecurity assessments are conducted annually; PDPL data breach response is automated with SDAIA notification workflow; risk appetite breach alerts are board-level
benchmarks.maturity_5: Adaptive fraud intelligence that learns from confirmed fraud patterns and adjusts models in near-real-time; zero false-negative target for high-value SARIE and mada fraud; NCA-certified cyber threat intelligence feeds are integrated with fraud and security operations; PDPL compliance is continuously monitored with automated data lineage and breach detection; operational risk management is fully integrated with risk appetite framework and capital planning; predictive KRI models anticipate operational risk events before they materialise; SAMA fraud reporting and NCA cybersecurity incident reporting are real-time via API
